risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The study's statistician used a computer random number generator to create a random number table in a nonblocked sequence. Participants were randomly assigned in equal numbers to the two groups." Comment: This explicitly describes a proper random sequence generation method (computer random number generator; random number table), so the allocation sequence is truly random.

Quote: "Allocation to the study group was concealed from the study coordinator and the participant until after the recruitment phone call. Following initial oral consent, the study coordinator consulted the allocation assignment table." Comment: This explicitly states that allocation was concealed from the recruiter until after enrollment-related contact, which prevents foreknowledge of the next assignment before the participant was enrolled.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants, physicians, and care managers could not be feasibly blinded to group assignment after randomization." Comment: This was an open randomized trial with no blinding of participants or personnel. The intervention involved active access to electronic medical records, secure messaging, and case-manager support, so knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence key outcomes such as glycemic control, patient satisfaction, and compliance.

Quote: "Study staff collecting outcomes were not blinded to group assignment." Comment: Although the study staff were unblinded, the main reported outcomes were objective, laboratory-based measures: "GHb values were rapid immunoassay tests from a Bayer Laboratories DCA-2000+ analyzer" and "Total cholesterol was used ... measured using enzymatic analytic chemistry"; the authors also note that "blood pressure measures were taken by medical staff not involved in the study." These measurements are unlikely to be influenced by knowledge of allocation, and no subjective patient-reported or clinician-judgement outcomes are central to this review question in the reported outcomes set.

Quote: "Intention-to-treat analysis of the main trial outcome included all randomly allocated participants with available outcome data. Three participants in the intervention group and six in the usual-care group did not have a follow-up GHb." Comment: There were 83 randomized participants (41 usual care, 42 intervention) and follow-up GHb was available for 35/41 (85.4%) in usual care and 39/42 (92.9%) in intervention, corresponding to attrition of 14.6% vs 7.1%. The between-arm difference in loss to follow-up is 7.5 percentage points, which exceeds the review's balanced-loss threshold for low risk and indicates differential attrition. Although overall loss is under 20% and the paper reports sensitivity analyses, the raw per-arm loss is not negligible and is imbalanced enough to raise attrition bias concerns.
